description: Workflow: apply a declarative JSON spec to create/modify an asset in one atomic call
text:
apply_spec is an operation on every session-based edit tool (behaviortree, blackboard, blueprint, eqs, niagara, pcg, statetree, widgetbp). It takes one JSON document describing the desired asset state and atomically applies it, replacing 35-60 sequential round-trips with a single call.

When to use it:
- Use incremental editing (add_node -> connect_pins -> save) when DISCOVERING what the graph should look like. Each step verifies; you find errors immediately.
- Use apply_spec once you have a verified shape, either by building incrementally first or from a known template.
- Pattern: discover incrementally, capture the shape as a spec, re-apply to confirm it reproduces.

Calling convention:
- Most tools nest params: behaviortree_edit(operation='apply_spec', params={'asset_path': '/Game/AI/BT_Foo', 'spec': {...}})
- Blueprint is flat: blueprint_edit_graph(operation='apply_spec', asset_path='/Game/BP_Foo', spec={...})
- Blueprint also creates missing assets; other tools require the target to exist (duplicate first).

Spec entry types by tool:
- behaviortree_edit: nodes[] (with parent, children, properties)
- blackboard_edit: keys[] (name, type)
- blueprint_edit_graph: nodes[], connections[], variables[]
- eqs_edit: options[] (generator, tests[])
- niagara_edit: parameters[] (emitters[] is a stub -- not implemented)
- pcg_edit: nodes[], connections[]
- statetree_edit: states[] plus per-state tasks/enter_conditions/transitions, plus top-level transitions[], evaluators[], global_tasks[]
- widgetbp_edit: widgets[] (parent, properties, slot_properties)

Key spec rules:
1. IDs are spec-local. Every entity gets a string 'id' you choose. Pass 1 creates entities and registers id_mappings[spec_id] -> actual_uid. Pass 2 wires relationships (parent, children, from, to) by looking up spec IDs in id_mappings.
2. Class names are fuzzy-resolved via ClaireonNameResolver::ResolveClassName. Drop U/F prefixes; partial matches work (BTComposite_Selector, K2Node_CustomEvent, EnvQueryGenerator_SimpleGrid).
3. Pin names are fuzzy-matched (exec -> execute, then -> then).
4. Spec is ADDITIVE on existing assets -- it does not replace. Applying a 'root selector' spec to a BT that already has one yields two trees under the root. Duplicate a clean template first if you need a fresh start.
5. BT property values go through FProperty::ImportText_Direct. FValueOrBBKey_* struct properties auto-wrap bare scalars as (DefaultValue=X), so 'WaitTime': '0.5' works. Other struct properties need full struct text format.
6. StateTree transitions can be authored per-state (states[].transitions[]) or top-level (transitions[] with from/to). Both resolve via id_mappings.

Atomicity:
- Spec validation failure returns before any mutation.
- All passes run inside FScopedTransaction.
- Critical error (asset becomes invalid) triggers GEditor->UndoTransaction().
- Per-entry failures (bad type, unknown property) are recorded in entries[] as 'failed' and accumulated as warnings. They do NOT rollback -- partial application of a 200-node spec is more useful than nothing.

Result shape:
{
  'data': {
    'id_mappings': {'spec_id_a': '<actual-guid-or-name>', ...},
    'entries': [{'spec_id': '...', 'status': 'ok|failed|skipped', 'actual_id': '...', 'error': '...'}]
  },
  'summary': 'apply_spec completed: N ok, M failed, K skipped',
  'warnings': [...]
}

Validation pattern when authoring a new spec:
1. Build incrementally with the session tool, inspect the result, capture structure.
2. Author an apply_spec for the same shape. Duplicate the template to a scratch path.
3. Apply the spec; assert summary starts with 'apply_spec completed:' and no entry has status 'failed'.
4. Re-open the scratch asset and compare its structure to the incremental capture.
5. Once a spec round-trips cleanly, use it as the canonical generator.

Current limitations:
- Niagara: emitters[] is a stub -- only parameters[] mutates the asset.
- WidgetBP: applicator opens existing widgets only; duplicate a template first.
- PCG: connection pin names are exact-match (no fuzzy resolution yet).
